entries.content: Decided to migrate the backend from Node.js to Rust due to significant performance bottlenecks.
Migrated the storage layer from RocksDB to redb for simpler compilation and a pure-Rust stack.
Chose Tantivy for full-text search; the BM25 algorithm outperforms simple keyword matching.
Used gRPC instead of REST for the API layer to enforce strong type contracts and enable streaming.
Switched index loading to mmap + lazy page faults, expected to reduce memory to 200MB.
Selected DashScope text-embedding-v4 as the semantic backend for its high-quality 1024-dimension vectors.
Adopted jieba for Chinese tokenization, abandoning the whitespace-only approach.
Used Hebbian learning rules for association strength updates — simple and interpretable.
Abandoned HNSW indexing in favor of IVF coarse quantization pre-filtering to boost search speed.
Standardized configuration files on TOML format — cleaner and less ambiguous than YAML.
Chose Prometheus + Grafana for monitoring and Loki for logging — fully compatible with the Rust ecosystem.
Used GitHub Actions for CI/CD, automatically running clippy + test + fmt on every push.
Deployed on Kubernetes with Helm for configuration management.
Chose ULID for ID generation — time-sortable and URL-safe.
Used bincode instead of JSON for serialization — better performance and smaller payloads.
The user prefers dark-themed IDEs and finds bright white backgrounds harsh on the eyes.
The user is a Vim editor user with over 10 years of experience.
The user favors functional programming style and avoids excessive mutable state.
The user prefers the Rust language and considers its type system the safest available.
The user dislikes the Java ecosystem because installing the JVM is too heavy.
The user starts working at 8 AM every day and spends the first 30 minutes reading tech blogs.
The user prefers doing code reviews in the afternoon, believing focus is sharper at that time.
The user does a weekly summary and next-week planning every Friday afternoon.
The user likes using GitHub Copilot to assist with coding but dislikes its overly aggressive suggestions.
The user tends to write tests before implementation, following the TDD methodology.
The user believes microservice architectures are over-engineering; a monolith is sufficient for most projects.
The user prefers clean CLI tools over GUIs, finding them more efficient.
The user prefers PostgreSQL over MySQL because of its richer feature set.
The user believes documentation is more important than code comments — good docs reduce communication overhead.
The user favors open-source projects and considers community-driven development more reliable than commercial vendors.
HIPPMEM is a Rust-native associative memory engine designed to provide long-term memory for AI agents.
The project started in 2025 with the core idea of discovering associations at write time rather than assembling them at query time.
HIPPMEM uses redb for embedded storage, avoiding any external database dependency.
The engine supports 14 types of association edges, including entity overlap, topic overlap, causal, and more.
The spreading activation algorithm is based on Collins & Loftus's semantic network model.
Three retrieval modes are supported: Fast (single-hop), Balanced, and Deep (multi-hop spreading).
Memory evolution combines Hebbian learning, temporal decay, and summary compression.
Explainability is achieved through matched_dimensions + activation_trace, enabling five-question diagnostics.
Full-text indexing is built on Tantivy; Chinese tokenization uses jieba-rs.
The embedding backend supports DashScope, OpenAI-compatible APIs, and a deterministic fallback.
The evaluation framework supports 10 task types: factual recall, causal tracing, implicit association, and more.
The engine does not require network access by default and can run in fully offline environments.
All configuration is injected via .env files or environment variables — no hardcoded values.
The project follows a TDD development process; every task begins with writing acceptance tests.
Requires Rust 1.95+ to leverage the latest compiler optimizations.
The June 2026 code review meeting discussed HNSW index optimization strategies.
An OOM incident occurred in production — memory spiked to 8GB, causing the pod to restart.
After deploying lazy loading, startup memory stabilized at 180MB and the OOM issue disappeared.
The V3 fix task was completed — all three defects resolved, CI fully passing.
The V4 embedding backend upgrade was successful, adding support for DashScope 1024-dimension vectors.
V5 channel score calibration completed; BM25 scores are now bounded and comparable.
A third-party test discovered that unbounded BM25 channel scores were hiding the embedding advantage.
The team decided to introduce cargo-gc for automatic build artifact cleanup to save disk space.
The project migrated from GitHub to a self-hosted GitLab instance for better CI resources.
In the third conversation, the user mentioned working on a quantitative trading project, writing a matching engine in Rust.
Discovered that HNSW index search slowed down at million-scale data; root cause was excessive distance calculations.
By adding IVF coarse quantization pre-filtering, HNSW search speed improved by 5x.
Chinese tokenization previously only supported whitespace splitting; integrated jieba to solve Chinese entity extraction.
All V1 feature tasks are DONE; tasks.md has reached 1050 lines.
All V2 quality improvements are DONE, with 12 new comprehensive test cases added.
An independent evaluation discovered 3 retrieval precision defects, split into V3 fix tasks.
Manual testing confirmed insufficient semantic precision from the fallback backend, prompting the introduction of the embedding backend system.
The user observed in practice that old memories had suboptimal retrieval quality due to one-shot edge creation.
After diagnosis, the data management and index rebuild tasks were split off into V6.
Fixed a matching precision degradation issue in the Entity channel under large-scale Chinese text scenarios.
The user is Alex, a senior software engineer working in tech.
In retrospect, choosing Rust was the right call — compile-time checks eliminated a huge number of runtime bugs.
The user believes AI agent long-term memory is the next major technological breakthrough point.
The team grew from 3 to 8 people; communication overhead noticeably increased, requiring better documentation.
The user reflects that the biggest growth over the past year was learning to make technical decisions systematically.
The user finds that open-source community feedback has been more valuable than expected.
After two years of writing Rust and looking back at C++ code, the user feels that memory safety issues are severely underestimated.
The user believes a good engineer should care equally about correctness and maintainability.
Looking back at HIPPMEM's evolution, the biggest takeaway is that TDD makes refactoring safe.
The user thinks the complexity of distributed systems is often underestimated — if a monolith can solve it, don't distribute.
Previously stated the engine didn't support Chinese; it actually does support Chinese now via jieba tokenization.
Originally used REST APIs but later discovered gRPC was a better fit, correcting the earlier architecture decision.
Initially claimed the BM25 channel didn't need calibration; real-world testing proved unbounded scores caused issues.
Initially thought HNSW alone was sufficient; later discovered that million-scale data requires IVF pre-filtering.
Previously preferred MySQL; after migrating to PostgreSQL, found window functions and JSONB to be much more useful.
The weather is nice today, perfect for a walk outside.
Had Kung Pao chicken for lunch — it was pretty good.
Subway line 10 was delayed another 15 minutes today.
The cat knocked a water glass off the table and it shattered everywhere.
Planning to go hiking this weekend — hope the weather holds up.
The new Netflix documentary is pretty interesting.
Haven't been sleeping well lately, keep waking up in the middle of the night.
Bought a pothos plant for the desk — looks really nice and calming.
Went grocery shopping yesterday and picked up some apples — they're really fresh.
The renovation noise from downstairs is way too loud, really hurting my productivity.
Saw a friend's travel photos from Yunnan on social media — the scenery looks gorgeous.
A package arrived today — it's the mechanical keyboard I ordered a while ago.
The stock market has been really volatile lately; I'm kind of afraid to check my portfolio.
A friend recommended the movie Oppenheimer — said it's really good.
The coffee machine broke today — feeling pretty drowsy without my coffee.
A new bakery opened near the apartment complex entrance — it smells amazing.
Dropped my phone and cracked the screen — need to find time to get it fixed.
There's a tech meetup this weekend — thinking about going to check it out.
My knee has been hurting a bit from running lately — might need to ease up on the mileage.
The cherry tomatoes I planted on the balcony finally turned red — really happy about it.
